text: The surgeon begins the incision on the right side of the upper mesorectum, dissecting the loose fibrous layer between the mesorectum and prehypogastric nerve fascia. Ventral traction is applied to the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, extending the fibrous tissues behind it. The peritoneum is incised up to the root of the IMA, which is then clipped and divided.

The surgeon begins the incision on the right side of the upper mesorectum, dissecting the loose fibrous layer between the mesorectum and prehypogastric nerve fascia. Ventral traction is applied to the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, extending the fibrous tissues behind it. The peritoneum is incised up to the root of the IMA, which is then clipped and divided.

The surgeon continues posterior dissection of the rectum, reaching the pelvic floor. The posterior side of the rectum is pushed upward, extending the loose fibrous layer. The right hypogastric nerve is visibly preserved during this process.

The surgeon continues posterior dissection of the rectum, pushing up the posterior side to extend the loose fibrous layer, while preserving the right hypogastric nerve. The dissection progresses until reaching the pelvic floor.

The surgeon divides the left hypogastric nerve and shifts the dissection layer to the outside. The left ureter is retracted from the external iliac artery and further separated from the internal iliac artery. Dissection continues along the medial wall, covering the ureter and hypogastric nerve.

The surgeon divides the left hypogastric nerve and shifts the dissection layer to the outside. The left ureter is retracted away from the external iliac artery and further separated from the internal iliac artery.

The surgeon continues dissection along the medial wall, revealing the ureter and hypogastric nerve. The superior vesicle artery is identified, followed by the internal iliac vein and inferior vesicle artery. Further dissection allows for clear visualization of the internal iliac vein and inferior vesicle vessels.
The surgeon identifies the superior vesicle artery, internal iliac vein, and inferior vesicle artery. Further dissection along the layer reveals the internal iliac vein and inferior vesicle vessels.

The surgeon identifies the tendinous arch of the levator ani muscle at the bottom of the surgical field. The prehypogastric nerve fascia is divided, and the rectum is lifted to allow for the division of the SIII and SIV pelvic splanchnic nerves on the left side.

The surgeon identifies the tendinous arch of the levator ani muscle at the bottom of the surgical field. The prehypogastric nerve fascia is divided, and the rectum is lifted to allow division of the SIII and SIV pelvic splanchnic nerves on the left side.

The surgeon preserves the SIII and SIV nerves on the right side while mobilizing the right side of the rectum along the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane. The anterior side of the rectum is visible, and the seminal vesicle is observed.
The surgeon preserves the SIII and SIV nerves on the right side while mobilizing the right side of the rectum along the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane.
The anterior side of the rectum is exposed, revealing the seminal vesicle.

The surgeon continues dissection on the right side, preserving the S-IV nerve branch and neurovascular bundle. The right side of the levator ani muscle is divided using electric cautery. The sacrum is partially divided using a soft coagulation method. The left side of the levator ani muscle is also divided to ensure adequate surgical margins, focusing on the left side of the rectum.

The surgeon continues dissection on the right side, preserving the S-IV nerve branch and neurovascular bundle. The right side of the levator ani muscle is divided using electric cautery. The sacrum is partially divided using a soft coagulation method. The left side of the levator ani muscle is also divided to ensure adequate surgical margins, focusing on the left side of the rectum.

The surgeon continues dissection on the left side of the rectum, carefully preserving the superior and inferior vesicle arteries. The levator ani muscle is divided, exposing the fat tissues in the ischiorectal fossa. The proximal sigmoid colon is then divided, and the patient's body position is adjusted to the jackknife position.

The surgeon continues dissection on the left side of the rectum, carefully preserving the superior and inferior vesicle arteries. Following the division of the levator ani muscle, the fat tissues in the ischiorectal fossa are exposed.

The surgeon divides the proximal sigmoid colon and then repositions the patient to the jackknife position. A transperineal approach is initiated, and sacrectomy is performed at the S5 level. The left side neurovascular bundle is resected under direct vision. The final view is shown from the transabdominal side.

The surgeon performs a sacrectomy at the level of S-five using the transperineal approach. The left side neurovascular bundle is resected under direct vision, providing a final view from the transabdominal side.
The surgeon visualizes the pelvic autonomic nerves, the cut end of the sacrum, and the divided levator ani muscle. The surgical field shows clear anatomical structures following resection.
The pelvic autonomic nerves are visible alongside the cut end of the sacrum and the divided levator ani muscle within the surgical field.
